branchingScenario.title: PBL: Male external genitalia — Clinical Reasoning

startScreen.startScreenSubtitle: Work through this clinical case to test your understanding of key concepts in male external genitalia anatomy. Make diagnostic and management decisions based on the clinical scenario.

endScreens.endScreenSubtitle: You made the correct clinical decisions at each step. You demonstrated strong understanding of the underlying concepts. Well done!
You showed some understanding but chose a suboptimal path. Review the feedback provided and try again to reinforce your learning.
Your clinical reasoning needs strengthening in this area. Review the source material carefully, paying attention to the key concepts highlighted, then attempt the case again.

params.text:
Case: Scrotal Swelling

A 20-year-old man presents with a painless left-sided scrotal swelling that feels like a "bag of worms" and is more prominent on standing. It disappears when he lies down. Examination reveals a left varicocele. The right side is normal.

Key concept: Varicocele is almost always left-sided because the left testicular vein drains into the left renal vein at a right angle, while the right testicular vein drains directly into the IVC.

branchingQuestion.question: Why is varicocele predominantly left-sided? What is the key anatomical difference in venous drainage?

alternatives.text: The left testicular vein drains into the left renal vein at a right angle; the right drains directly into the IVC

feedback.title: Correct!
feedback.subtitle: The right-angle drainage into the left renal vein creates higher venous pressure, predisposing to varicocele on the left side.

alternatives.text: The left testis hangs lower, creating more hydrostatic pressure in its veins

feedback.title: Incomplete
feedback.subtitle: While the left testis does hang lower, the key anatomical reason is the venous drainage pattern — left testicular vein enters the left renal vein at a right angle.

alternatives.text: The left testis has more veins in the pampiniform plexus than the right

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: Both sides have similar pampiniform plexuses. The left-sided predominance is due to the left testicular vein draining into the renal vein at right angles.

params.text:
Further Evaluation

Semen analysis shows reduced sperm count. Ultrasound confirms Grade III varicocele. The urologist discusses the anatomy of the testis — it is enclosed by the tunica albuginea and divided into approximately 250 lobules containing seminiferous tubules. He explains that the testis developed retroperitoneally.

Concept: The lymphatic drainage of the testis is to the para-aortic (lumbar) nodes, not inguinal nodes, because the testis develops retroperitoneally and descends through the inguinal canal guided by the gubernaculum.

branchingQuestion.question: If a testicular tumour is suspected, where would you expect lymph node metastasis? Why not to inguinal nodes?

alternatives.text: Para-aortic (lumbar) nodes — because the testis develops retroperitoneally and descends guided by the gubernaculum

feedback.title: Correct!
feedback.subtitle: Testicular lymphatics follow the developmental origin. The testis develops retroperitoneally, so its lymphatics drain to para-aortic nodes, not inguinal.

alternatives.text: Superficial inguinal nodes — because the testis lies in the scrotum

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: The scrotal skin drains to inguinal nodes, but the testis itself drains to para-aortic nodes reflecting its retroperitoneal developmental origin.

alternatives.text: External iliac nodes — following the cremasteric artery drainage

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: The testicular lymphatic drainage follows the testicular vessels to para-aortic nodes, reflecting the gonad's retroperitoneal origin before descent.

params.text:
Clinical Resolution

Varicocelectomy is performed. Follow-up semen analysis shows improvement in sperm parameters. No testicular tumour is identified.

Learning points:

  • Testis: enclosed by tunica albuginea, ~250 lobules with seminiferous tubules
  • Cremasteric muscle from internal oblique — thermoregulation
  • Left varicocele: left testicular vein drains into left renal vein (right angle)
  • Testicular lymphatics drain to para-aortic nodes (retroperitoneal origin)
  • Testicular descent guided by the gubernaculum
